_description: Narrative style and tropes researched from Herge's 'The Adventures of Tintin'. Produced by the Style Research agent (Phase 0.5) to serve as thematic ingredients for the downstream lore merging step.

thematic_tropes.name: Accidental Involvement & Mistaken Identity
thematic_tropes.description: The protagonist is drawn into a massive, dangerous plot not by choice or destiny, but by a random accident: buying a model ship, picking up a dropped wallet, or being mistaken for a foreign agent.
thematic_tropes.example_usage: Tintin purchases a model of the Unicorn ship at a market, which contains a secret scroll, drawing the attention of dangerous collectors and drawing him into a hunt for pirate treasure.

thematic_tropes.name: The Mysterious Clue or MacGuffin Hunt
thematic_tropes.description: The story revolves around a physical object or puzzle (a scroll, a scepter, a falling meteorite, a stolen statue) that the protagonist must find, decipher, or protect from rival factions.
thematic_tropes.example_usage: The search for King Ottokar's Scepter, which must be returned to the king before a national holiday to prevent a political coup.

thematic_tropes.name: Global Travel & Multi-Locale Adventure
thematic_tropes.description: The narrative scale shifts rapidly from a quiet domestic setting (Marlinstike Hall) to multiple international, often exotic, locales (e.g. desert, mountains, foreign cities, space) to track down the mystery.
thematic_tropes.example_usage: The pursuit of opium smugglers leads Tintin from Belgium to Egypt, India, and eventually China.

thematic_tropes.name: Shadowy Syndicate / Conspiracy
thematic_tropes.description: The villains are not lone wolves but belong to a highly organized, international conspiracy: smugglers, counterfeiters, secret societies, or military juntas operating behind respectable facades.
thematic_tropes.example_usage: The international drug ring led by Rastapopoulos operating under the cover of a movie production company.

narrative_rules.reason: The protagonist has no hidden selfish motives or psychological darkness. They act purely out of bravery, loyalty, and the desire to help the weak and bring criminals to justice.
There are no deep romantic subplots or internal psychological monologues for human characters. Tension comes from external dangers, puzzles, and physical obstacles.
The dog companion acts as the moral and cynical proxy for the reader, using thought balloons to offer commentary, express fear, or warn the protagonist of things they missed.
Tense action beats or dramatic plot twists are immediately followed by or mixed with physical comedy (slapstick) or character-based humor to keep the tone light and family-friendly.

humor_and_pacing_tropes: Slapstick collisions, falls, and vehicle crashes
Running gags (e.g. wrong phone numbers, recurring annoying callers)
Professor Calculus misinterpreting normal questions as insults
Bowler-hatted detectives repeating each other's phrases redundantly
